skill.name: routeros-fundamentals
skill.description: RouterOS v7 domain knowledge for AI agents. Use when: working with MikroTik RouterOS, writing RouterOS CLI/script commands, calling RouterOS REST API, debugging why a Linux command fails on RouterOS, or when the user mentions MikroTik, RouterOS, CHR, or /ip /system /interface paths. Scope: RouterOS 7.x (long-term and newer) only — v6 is NOT covered and accuracy for v6 problems will be low.
skill.summary: Domain knowledge for MikroTik RouterOS v7 — CLI syntax, REST API patterns, scripting, firewall, networking, and versioning for AI agents
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill providing RouterOS v7 domain knowledge. All 1893 static findings are false positives: backtick patterns are code examples in markdown documentation, not executable code; weak crypto references are cipher suite names in configuration examples; credential patterns are documentation references. No malicious code execution patterns exist. Skill is safe to publish.

medium_findings.title: External Command Pattern in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Markdown documentation contains backtick-delimited code examples showing RouterOS CLI commands that resemble shell backtick syntax. These are documentation examples, not executable code. The skill is documentation-only with no code execution capability.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern detection triggers on markdown code blocks due to backtick syntax. However, these are documentation examples of RouterOS CLI commands, not executable shell code. The skill contains no code execution functionality.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Documentation contains hardcoded URLs and IP addresses for demonstration purposes (e.g., 127.0.0.1:9100, example.com). These are example values for documentation clarity, not actual network connections.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs/IPs in documentation are example placeholders, not active network connections. No actual network requests are made by the skill.

low_findings.title: Credential References in Documentation
low_findings.description: Documentation references default credentials (admin:) and SSH key paths (id_ed25519.pub) for educational purposes. These are documentation patterns, not actual credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential references are documentation examples showing RouterOS REST API authentication patterns. No actual credentials are hardcoded in the skill.

low_findings.title: Version Parsing with Crypto Keywords
low_findings.description: Version-parsing code includes strings like 'des-cbc', 'rc4', 'md5' in cipher suite documentation. These are RouterOS configuration values, not actual crypto operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Crypto keywords appear in RouterOS cipher suite documentation. This is documentation of RouterOS configuration options, not implementation of weak crypto.

content.value_statement: AI agents often fail when working with RouterOS because it uses a proprietary CLI, not Linux commands. This skill provides the domain knowledge to write correct RouterOS commands, REST API calls, and scripts without Linux hallucinations.
actual_capabilities: Translate between Linux shell commands and RouterOS CLI equivalents
Generate correct RouterOS REST API calls with proper HTTP verb mapping
Write RouterOS scripts (.rsc files) using the proprietary scripting language
Describe RouterOS version schemes and help with upgrade planning
Identify RouterOS vs Linux differences to prevent common agent mistakes
limitations: Only covers RouterOS 7.x — accuracy is low for RouterOS v6
Does not execute commands on actual routers — provides guidance only
Does not include device-specific features not in mainstream RouterOS
Cannot replace formal RouterOS certification or hands-on experience

use_cases.title: Network Automation Engineer configuring RouterOS via API
use_cases.description: Generate and validate REST API calls for configuring firewall rules, VLANs, routing, and user management on MikroTik devices. Ensures correct HTTP verbs (PUT=create, PATCH=update) and proper JSON formatting.

use_cases.title: Developer debugging RouterOS CLI commands
use_cases.description: Get correct RouterOS CLI syntax when SSH'd into a router or writing automation scripts. Avoids common mistakes like using Linux commands that do not exist on RouterOS.

use_cases.title: AI Agent requiring RouterOS context
use_cases.description: Provide RouterOS domain knowledge to an AI agent so it can correctly interpret user requests about MikroTik, write appropriate commands, and avoid Linux-hallucination mistakes.
use_cases.target_user: AI agents and Claude Code users

prompt_templates.title: Basic: RouterOS CLI command lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I {task} in RouterOS CLI? For example: list all interfaces, add an IP address, enable a firewall rule.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a RouterOS CLI command and explanation

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: REST API request generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a REST API request to {resource} with {properties} on RouterOS. Show the HTTP method, URL path, and JSON body.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs API code for RouterOS automation

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Script generation with error handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a RouterOS script that {description}. Include proper error handling, variable declarations, and comments. Show the equivalent CLI commands too.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a complete RouterOS script with validation

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Multi-step configuration task
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan the steps to configure {feature} on RouterOS. For each step, show the CLI command and REST API equivalent. Include prerequisites and validation commands.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a complete configuration guide

output_examples.input: How do I add an IP address to interface ether1?
output: RouterOS uses = for assignment (no spaces), [find] for queries, and "" for strings.
RouterOS does not have Linux coreutils. The 'ls' command does not exist.
RouterOS CLI equivalents: /file/print (list files), /interface/print (list interfaces), /ip/address/print (list IPs)
RouterOS runs a proprietary 'nova' system above the Linux kernel, not standard Linux.

best_practices: Always verify RouterOS version before applying commands — v6 and v7 have significant differences
Use [find] expressions instead of hardcoded IDs when writing scripts that may run on multiple routers
Test REST API calls with "once":"" parameter first to avoid blocking HTTP calls
Document custom scripts in RouterOS with :doc comments for future reference
anti_patterns: Do not suggest Linux commands (apt, ls, cat, grep) on RouterOS — they do not exist
Do not use Unix shell syntax ($variable, | pipe, subshells) in RouterOS scripts
Do not assume /etc or standard Linux paths exist — RouterOS has its own configuration database

faq.question: Does this skill work with RouterOS v6?
faq.answer: No. This skill covers RouterOS 7.x only. Accuracy for v6 is low because many commands and APIs differ significantly between versions.

faq.question: Can this skill execute commands on my router?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides documentation and guidance only. It cannot connect to routers or execute commands. Use the RouterOS REST API directly for automation.

faq.question: What is the difference between RouterOS CLI and Linux shell?
faq.answer: RouterOS CLI is a proprietary command language with path-based navigation (/ip/address/print), not Unix shell. It has no pipes, no subshells, and no standard Linux utilities.

faq.question: Why does PUT create instead of update in RouterOS REST API?
faq.answer: RouterOS REST uses non-standard HTTP verb mapping: PUT creates new entries, PATCH modifies existing entries. This differs from typical REST APIs where PUT updates resources.

faq.question: How do I handle RouterOS authentication in REST API calls?
faq.answer: Use HTTP Basic Auth with the format 'username:' (colon, no password for default admin). Example: curl -u admin: http://router-ip/rest/ip/address

faq.question: Can this skill help with RouterOS scripting?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill covers RouterOS scripting language syntax including :local variables, :put commands, :if conditionals, :foreach loops, and string operations using :pick, :find, :len.
